LUDUS.

canniliciuscore

Summary:

What if you were different? Would you still stay?

We cannot force anyone to remain. But when the person who matters most chooses to leave, it’s impossible not to wonder whether things might have turned out differently under other circumstances. If they had looked beyond what seemed like selfishness. If they had understood that perhaps walking away was never an act of indifference, but a desperate attempt to protect the person they loved.

This is a story about two people who found each other when no one else could truly understand them and who, because of that, were destined to change one another forever. It is a story about admiration turning into attachment, affection threatening to become something more, and choices born from fear rather than reason. Because loving someone does not always mean accepting them, and sometimes the brightest souls are also the most terrified when forced to confront what they truly feel.

Perhaps the real tragedy is that, at one point, they could have been something more—and neither of them had the courage to find out what that was.

Chapter 1: 221 B

Chapter Text

English isn't my native language, so please excuse my Neanderthal accent.

For my Latin American readers, I've published this same story in Spanish on Wattpad, under the same username.

Hugs and kisses, and thanks for reading!

— Liesel.

 

“Doesn’t it feel welcoming, James?”

The excitement in his voice was almost indecent for a place like this. He stood in the middle of the sitting room with his arms spread wide, a smile far too large for his face—the smile of a child on Christmas morning, even if Christmas happened to be celebrated in a room that smelled faintly of a tomb. Old dust, damp wood, and something vaguely resembling mothballs mingled with the perpetual soot London seemed determined to settle over every surface. It was the scent of a place that had once been lived in, then forgotten, and had resigned itself to remaining so.

Sherlock Holmes, however, crossed the threshold as though entering a sanctuary. James Moriarty did not answer at once. He closed the door carefully behind him and allowed himself a moment to observe. The Baker Street apartment was small in a manner that bordered on offensive. The sitting room, if it deserved the name, could have fit twice inside one of the guest bedrooms in the estate where Sherlock had grown up beneath the shadow of Silas Holmes. The wallpaper belonged to another decade, faded into uneven patches wherever dampness had won its own private battles. A crack ran across the ceiling from the cornice almost to the centre of the room. The chandelier hanging above was wrought iron, darkened with age, its arms still bearing hardened traces of white candle wax—evidence either of someone who had spent a very long time waiting in that room, or of someone who had simply forgotten to extinguish the lights.

The furniture slept beneath sheets. Not elegant white sheets, but grey ones, stained along the edges, their folds suggesting they had remained untouched for years. Moriarty nudged the corner of the Persian rug resting at the centre of the floor with the tip of his shoe. In better days, it might have possessed a certain dignity. The red still struggled to survive beneath the dust, woven between geometric patterns nearly erased by footsteps and time alike.

“So this is what you bought with the portion of Silas’s inheritance?” The Irishman let his gaze travel from the Briton to the chandelier, then to the rug. “I rented it, James. Be grateful Mrs. Hudson is rather fond of me.”

“Why?” Moriarty arched an eyebrow. “Because you entertained her after her husband died?”

Sherlock exhaled through his nose. The hands he had held theatrically open at his sides slowly fell. James Moriarty possessed a peculiar talent for approaching a subject from an angle no one quite anticipated. His remarks were not cruel, exactly. They were precise in a way that often proved more uncomfortable than outright cruelty.

“How long is the lease?” Moriarty resumed his slow inspection, moving toward the hallway leading deeper into the flat, where two half-open doors promised rooms of equally austere character.

“Six months. Starting this week.”

“And the cost?”

“Entirely irrelevant, my dear James.”

Moriarty stopped and looked at him. His expression was half disbelief and half something that, in another man, might have been called affection.

“Six months to settle in and improve…” He paused deliberately, eyes fixed upon the crack in the ceiling. “…the view.” Then he looked back at Sherlock. “Which means we shall need to move quickly and earn enough money to continue funding Your Majesty’s obsession with space and privacy. How exactly do you intend to accomplish that?” Sherlock’s smile widened almost involuntarily.

“If my analysis is correct,” he said, and there was something in his voice that was not quite certainty but rather the anticipation of it, “we have six months to make this place our own. And to solve whatever the honourable Mr. Holmes was kind enough to leave me before he died.”

“And find employment,” Moriarty added in the same tone one might use to remind a man that it was still raining outside. “Oxford is no longer an option, if you recall.” Sherlock inclined his head and pressed his palms together in a small, theatrical bow.

“My, how clever you are, Moriarty.” He straightened. “Let the present decide the future.” Before the Irishman could respond, Sherlock seized the nearest sheet—the one covering what proved to be a massive dark mahogany armchair—and pulled it away with a sharp flourish. The dust awoke at once. It rose in a dense golden cloud beneath the meagre light filtering through the dirty window, suspended in the air as though the house’s accumulated years had decided to make themselves visible for a fleeting moment. Moriarty watched for a second. Then he sighed, removed his gloves, and went in search of the next sheet.

Only a few months had passed since Silas Holmes’s death, and the shock had travelled through them in very different ways. For Sherlock, it had been like an electrical current releasing something long held captive. For Moriarty, it had been the quiet confirmation of something he had known for years and wished he had never known at all. In those days, the Briton still possessed that particular kind of enthusiasm that can only survive before one has accumulated enough consequences of one’s own. His childhood, irregular though it had been, had left something essential untouched: an almost physical curiosity about the world, a tendency to see doors where others saw walls, and the dangerous habit of opening them without asking what might lie on the other side. What Silas Holmes had left him was a knot of unanswered questions, and Sherlock carried it with him like an old map—equal parts frustration and fascination.

At the same time, the Irishman bore a concern as intrinsic to him as the colour of his eyes. The sort that comes from having already seen the bottom of something and having no desire to return.
Folded carefully within the inner pocket of his coat still rested that scrap of paper bearing a curse written in the language of mathematics. He kept it for contingencies. In case the money ran out before something better appeared. In case the perfect opportunity presented itself. In case Sherlock Holmes decided, as was his custom, that danger sounded like an excellent idea. And although the Englishman knew about that particular detail, he had chosen to indulge in a measure of naïveté and refrain from confessing that he himself carried a key capable of undoing everything.

They lied to each other. Or rather, they concealed fragments of truths the other had already begun to suspect. But in that sitting room still clouded with dust, with the dark mahogany finally visible and London rumbling distantly three floors below, lying felt safer than the alternative. The complete truth, unfiltered and whole, looked very much like something that, once spoken aloud, could never be contained again. And neither of them was ready—not yet—for whatever would come after the last of it had been said.
